title: Dune Chapel Music Festival
creator: Various Artists
locality_note: Held in the Indiana Dunes along Lake Michigan, the festival uses the natural bowl of the dunes as an open-air concert hall for sacred and contemplative music.

title: Indianapolis Museum of Art at Newfields
creator: Various
locality_note: One of the largest encyclopaedic art museums in the US, Newfields pairs fine art galleries with a landscape that shifts with seasons—a distinctly Hoosier combination of culture and land.

title: Tecumseh! Outdoor Drama
creator: Allan Eckert
themes: the Ohio Valley as contested land
locality_note: Performed in a natural hillside amphitheatre near Chillicothe, Ohio—just across the Indiana border—this drama centres the Shawnee leader Tecumseh's resistance against American expansion across the Old Northwest.

title: A Girl Is a Half-formed Thing
creator: Eimear McBride
themes: the body as site of violation
locality_note: Though Irish in origin, this novel is widely taught at Indiana's creative writing programs and resonates with the literary culture of Midwest university towns like Bloomington, where IU's MFA community engages it as a touchstone of radical form.

title: Hoagy Carmichael: Stardust
creator: Hoagy Carmichael
themes: the American popular song as emotional autobiography
locality_note: Bloomington-born Carmichael wrote 'Stardust' while at Indiana University, making this the quintessential Indiana musical export — a piece of longing so universal it became the most recorded song in American history.

title: Fried Green Tomatoes at the Whistle Stop Cafe
creator: Fannie Flagg
themes: the Jim Crow South and its violence
the past alive in the present
aesthetic: the diner as crossroads of community life
locality_note: Set in the fictional Whistle Stop, Alabama—modelled on Irondale near Birmingham, Georgia—the novel weaves the Black and white lives of the Deep South into a story of love and survival that Georgia has claimed as cultural kin.

title: Midnight in the Garden of Good and Evil
creator: John Berendt
themes: the seductive decadence of the Old South
aesthetic: Savannah's Spanish moss and Federal architecture
locality_note: Set entirely in Savannah, Georgia, this book turned the city's squares, mansions, and Bonaventure Cemetery into the world's most literarily famous Southern Gothic stage.

title: Outkast: ATLiens
creator: Outkast
themes: Black Southern identity remade as cosmic
the outsider who transforms the culture
locality_note: Recorded and set in Atlanta, ATLiens reimagines the city not as Southern rap centre but as alien spacecraft — a vision of Black Southern creativity that is impossible to separate from Atlanta's unique cultural geography.

title: Beloved (film)
creator: Jonathan Demme
themes: the community that casts out and reclaims
aesthetic: Bluestone Road as the geography of trauma
locality_note: Though set in Cincinnati, the film was shot partly in Georgia, and its cultural resonance in the state — where slavery's legacy saturates the landscape — makes it essential viewing in any serious Georgia humanities context.

title: Selma
creator: Ava DuVernay
themes: the courage of non-violent direct action
the Edmund Pettus Bridge as American crossroads
locality_note: Directed by Atlanta-raised Ava DuVernay and largely filmed in Georgia, Selma is both a portrait of Alabama 1965 and a deeply Georgian film about the Black South's ongoing struggle for dignity.

title: Chinatown
creator: Roman Polanski
themes: power and corruption beneath civic idealism
water as political weapon in the West
cynicism as the only honest response
locality_note: A film about Los Angeles that uses the real Owens Valley water wars as its political bedrock — the California aqueduct as moral crime — Chinatown is inseparable from the state's history of resource colonialism.

title: The Grapes of Wrath
creator: John Steinbeck
themes: displacement and the broken American Dream
dignity in the face of exploitation
locality_note: Steinbeck's California — the San Joaquin Valley, the Salinas Valley, the labour camps of Tulare County — is the moral landscape of this novel, a state that promised everything and delivered exploitation.

title: Channel Orange
creator: Frank Ocean
themes: Los Angeles as dream and disillusionment
nostalgia for a summer that never returns
aesthetic: the pop cultural landscape as emotional text
locality_note: A deeply Californian album, Channel Orange maps Los Angeles — its freeways, its summer light, its fantasy of reinvention — onto an emotional landscape of longing and queer desire that feels specific to the city.

title: Inherent Vice
creator: Thomas Pynchon
locality_note: Set in the fictional Gordita Beach (Manhattan Beach, California), Inherent Vice is Pynchon's love letter and elegy to a California that the Nixon era ended — the surf-and-dope idyll swallowed by the surveillance state.

title: A Tree Grows in Brooklyn
creator: Betty Smith
themes: poverty and the dream of education
female resilience and coming of age
locality_note: Set entirely in Williamsburg, Brooklyn, the novel is inseparable from the neighbourhood — the local library, the tenement backyard, the Nolan family's specific corner of immigrant New York circa 1900–1919.

title: The Velvet Underground & Nico
creator: The Velvet Underground
themes: underground New York and its extremes
locality_note: Born entirely from the downtown Manhattan milieu of Warhol's Factory on 47th Street, this album could not exist without New York's specific 1966 underground — its drag queens, junkies, and downtown artists.

title: Rent
creator: Jonathan Larson
themes: the urgency of the present moment
locality_note: Set entirely in Alphabet City, Manhattan's East Village, Rent maps the specific geography of AIDS-era lower Manhattan — Tompkins Square Park, the corner of Avenue B, the life and death of a community.

title: Giovanni's Room
creator: James Baldwin
themes: the cost of denying the self
the inevitability of tragedy when authenticity is refused
locality_note: Though set in Paris, Baldwin wrote this in New York and it belongs to the Black queer intellectual tradition of Harlem and Greenwich Village — the dual exile of being Black and gay in mid-century America that only New York's underground could shelter.

title: Louisiana Story
creator: Robert Flaherty
themes: the bayou as sacred wild space
a child's encounter with industrial modernity
locality_note: Filmed entirely in the Cajun bayous of Vermilion Parish, Louisiana, the film is one of cinema's most lyrical documents of Louisiana's swamp landscape and the Cajun world of the Atchafalaya basin.

title: A Streetcar Named Desire
creator: Tennessee Williams
themes: the death of the Old South in the New
locality_note: Set on Elysian Fields Avenue in the Faubourg Marigny neighbourhood of New Orleans, Williams drew on his own years living in the French Quarter to create the definitive literary portrait of New Orleans as erotic, brutal, and mythic.

title: Treme
creator: David Simon
themes: New Orleans cultural specificity as American treasure
locality_note: Named for the Treme neighbourhood of New Orleans — the oldest African American neighbourhood in the US — this series documents post-Katrina recovery through the lens of New Orleans' irreplaceable music culture.

title: Their Eyes Were Watching God
creator: Zora Neale Hurston
themes: the Black Southern community as complex world
locality_note: Though set in Eatonville, Florida, Hurston's cultural roots ran through the Black Gulf South including Louisiana's Creole belt, and the novel is essential reading in Louisiana's Black literary tradition — the bayou and hammock country is kin to her swamps.

title: Bless Me, Ultima
creator: Rudolfo Anaya
themes: spiritual knowledge transmitted through the old
the New Mexico llano as sacred space
coming of age through moral crisis
the curandera as keeper of memory
aesthetic: the Golden Carp as pagan god
locality_note: Set in Guadalupe, New Mexico during WWII, Anaya's novel is inseparable from the llano — the high plains east of Santa Fe — and the Chicano Catholic villages of the upper Rio Grande valley where indigenous and Spanish spiritualities merged.

title: Georgia O'Keeffe: Ghost Ranch
creator: Georgia O'Keeffe
themes: the feminine in the desert landscape
bones and flowers as the same thing
the New Mexico sky as subject
locality_note: O'Keeffe's Ghost Ranch studio near Abiquiu, New Mexico is both her home and her subject — the red hills, bleached skulls, and blue sky of the Piedra Lumbre basin become the content of her canvases over four decades.

title: House Made of Dawn
creator: N. Scott Momaday
themes: the Native veteran caught between worlds
the Jemez Pueblo as spiritual home
trauma and the loss of cultural grounding
the land as the only true medicine
locality_note: Set in the Jemez Pueblo of New Mexico, Momaday draws on his own Kiowa-Jemez heritage to create a portrait of the high desert village as the only ground where healing is possible — and the city as spiritual death.

title: Breaking Bad
creator: Vince Gilligan
themes: the American Southwest as moral wilderness
the meth economy of the borderlands
a man's transformation into what he was always becoming
aesthetic: the RV as freedom and trap
locality_note: Filmed entirely in Albuquerque, Breaking Bad uses New Mexico's high desert light, its strip mall sprawl, and its proximity to the border to construct a uniquely Southwestern moral landscape for Walter White's descent.

title: A Prairie Home Companion
creator: Garrison Keillor
themes: small-town Lutheran life and its gentle absurdities
locality_note: Broadcast from the Fitzgerald Theatre in Saint Paul and rooted in Keillor's Stearns County Minnesota upbringing, the show created Lake Wobegon as the definitive literary landscape of Minnesota's small-town Lutheran Norwegian culture.

title: Purple Rain
creator: Prince
themes: Minneapolis club culture as spiritual arena
locality_note: Recorded at Prince's Paisley Park studio in Chanhassen, Minnesota and set in First Avenue nightclub in Minneapolis, Purple Rain is the definitive Minneapolis cultural document — a city's sound, grief, and ecstasy in one film and album.

title: Fargo
creator: Joel and Ethan Coen
themes: evil erupting in the ordinary Midwest
the pregnant detective as moral centre
the Minnesota nice that is not naive
aesthetic: the ordinary and the horrifying side by side
locality_note: Shot in Brainerd, Minnesota and Fargo, North Dakota, the Coens use the specific visual culture of the Upper Midwest — snow, diners, Scandinavian politeness — to frame one of American cinema's most distinctive moral landscapes.

title: The Spoon River Anthology
creator: Edgar Lee Masters
themes: the secrets of the ordinary dead
the Midwest small town and its buried lives
the confession the living could not make
community as lattice of hidden stories
locality_note: Though set in the fictional Spoon River, Illinois, this anthology speaks directly to the Minnesota River valley towns and their buried secrets — widely read in Minnesota high schools as the voice of the Midwest's hidden interior lives.

title: True Women
creator: Janice Woods Windle
themes: the Comanche wars and settler violence
the founding of Texas through women's eyes
survival and the cost of civilization
aesthetic: Comanche raid and Republic of Texas drama
locality_note: Based on the real diaries of the Woods-DeWees-Lawshe families of Gonzales, Texas, this novel is a literary excavation of the Texas Hill Country's founding women — the state's hidden matriarchal history.

title: Lonesome Dove
creator: Larry McMurtry
themes: the last great cattle drive as elegy
the myth and reality of the West
the cost of wandering without roots
the Texas Ranger as dying type
aesthetic: Texas to Montana cattle trail realism
the plains as endless moral test
the Western as tragedy not triumph
locality_note: Beginning in the fictional Lonesome Dove on the Rio Grande (based on Eagle Pass, Texas), McMurtry's epic is the definitive literary portrait of the post-Civil War Texas trail drives — the state's mythology examined with clear eyes.

title: Townes Van Zandt: Live at the Old Quarter
creator: Townes Van Zandt
themes: the cosmic loneliness of the Texas singer-songwriter
the beautiful tragedy of being alive
aesthetic: the ghost in every love song
locality_note: Recorded over two nights at the Old Quarter club in Houston's Midtown neighbourhood in 1973, this is the definitive document of the Texas singer-songwriter scene that produced Townes, Guy Clark, Steve Earle, and Lyle Lovett.

title: No Country for Old Men
creator: Cormac McCarthy
themes: the old order unable to understand modernity's violence
fate and the impossibility of escape
the West Texas border as moral frontier
what we pass on to those who come after
aesthetic: Anton Chigurh as force of nature
locality_note: Set along the Texas-Mexico border from Del Rio to Eagle Pass to the Trans-Pecos region, McCarthy's novel uses the specific geography of the Big Bend country — remote, violent, beautiful — as the stage for his meditation on evil and fate.

title: Portlandia
creator: Fred Armisen and Carrie Brownstein
themes: the absurdity of progressive urban identity
Portland's hipster culture and its self-parody
the Pacific Northwest as alternative universe
locality_note: Filmed almost entirely on location in Portland, Oregon's inner Southeast and Northeast neighbourhoods, this show elevated specific Portland streets, shops, and cultural tics into a globally recognised brand of Pacific Northwest self-parody.

title: The Joy Luck Club
creator: Amy Tan
themes: the chasm between immigrant mothers and American daughters
the stories that are never told and always felt
San Francisco's Chinatown as portal between worlds
aesthetic: Chinese oral folktale woven into realism
the kitchen as site of cultural transmission
locality_note: Set in San Francisco's Richmond and Sunset Districts — the heart of Chinese American San Francisco — Tan's novel maps the community's geography from Chinatown restaurants to suburban living rooms to the women's country of origin.

title: Jazz at Lincoln Center
creator: Various Artists
themes: jazz as living American art form
the concert hall meeting the blues
Wynton Marsalis's vision of jazz as democratic art
the preservation and transformation of tradition
New York as the world's jazz capital
aesthetic: Frederick P. Rose Hall in Columbus Circle
the swing of the Big Band tradition
locality_note: Located at Columbus Circle in Manhattan, Jazz at Lincoln Center is both a performance hall and a cultural argument — Wynton Marsalis's insistence that jazz is America's classical music, and New York is its natural home.

title: Mud
creator: Jeff Nichols
themes: romantic love as myth and burden
aesthetic: the river as freedom and danger
locality_note: Filmed on and around the actual Mississippi River islands of Arkansas — the border territory between Arkansas and Louisiana — Mud is a cinematic portrait of river life that feels rooted in the bayou culture of the Louisiana-Arkansas delta.

title: O Brother, Where Art Thou?
creator: Joel and Ethan Coen
themes: the Southern folk music tradition as Odyssey
the Depression-era South's music and mythology
blind prophets and Sirens in Mississippi
aesthetic: the chain gang as Greek chorus
the Soggy Bottom Boys as Homeric heroes
locality_note: Filmed primarily in Mississippi and North Georgia's rural counties, the Coens use the actual landscapes of the Deep South's piney woods and red clay to build their mythological Southern Odyssey.

title: Minnesota Nice: A History of Race in the Twin Cities
creator: Myron Medcalf
themes: racial inequality beneath the surface of civic kindness
the Twin Cities Black community's erasure and persistence
the gap between progressive identity and lived reality
aesthetic: the uncomfortable truth beneath the nice
locality_note: A deep dive into the racial geography of the Twin Cities — North Minneapolis, Rondo, and the suburbs of the metro area — this book reads the specific history of Minnesota's racial contradictions as a national story.

title: When the Emperor Was Divine
creator: Julie Otsuka
themes: the erasure of the self under state violence
return to a home that has become foreign
the silence that descends on a family after trauma
California's complicity in a national crime
aesthetic: Berkeley California and Topaz Utah internment camp
locality_note: Set in Berkeley, California — where the family is forcibly removed from — and in the Topaz internment camp in Utah, this novel is essential to understanding California's own history of racial violence against its Japanese American residents.
